title: TBM 399: 10 Prioritization Traps
questions: How do we stop spending all our time on urgent, low-to-medium value work?
What should we do when teams keep refining work but ship too late to matter?
How can leadership ensure a “top priority” actually gets focus and trade-offs?
How do we measure and prevent hidden friction that slows everything down?
search_terms: cost of delay vs tech cleanup
summary: This post lays out ten prioritization anti-patterns that commonly derail startups and product/engineering teams, from reactive “burning down the house” work to “someday never comes” forever problems. It’s aimed at leaders and teams who need practical ways to make better trade-offs under time pressure, using concrete interventions like protected capacity, early de-risking, explicit drop rules, off-track signals, and learning-focused experiments.

title: TBM 384: Prioritization Starts With Strategic Prioritization
questions: How do we prioritize when we don’t have a real strategy behind the work?
Which benefit type (increase/protect revenue, decrease/protect costs) does each initiative actually serve?
Where are our advantages decaying, and what leverage are our bets creating or destroying?
Which strategic powers should we build or reinforce, and how does that change across the advantage lifecycle?
search_terms: four benefit types increase protect revenue decrease protect costs
lifecycle of strategic advantage discovery growth extraction erosion
portfolio of strategic bets discovery growth extraction
Wardley Maps strategy conversations where to play how to win
prioritization criteria magnitude timing confidence opportunity cost
summary: The post explains how to connect strategy to prioritization by grounding “value” and “urgency” in business benefit types, strategic drivers, and the forces of decay and leverage. It’s aimed at product and strategy teams who need a practical way to choose between initiatives by mapping bets to durable advantage (Helmer’s 7 powers) and to the lifecycle of strategic advantage, then ranking work using impact, timing, confidence, and opportunity cost.

title: TBM 381: Stop Trying To Make Prioritization "Easy"
questions: How do we prioritize goals, objectives, and strategies given fixed capacity?
Are we optimizing the wrong thing—ambition, capacity fit, or new investment—at the expense of impact?
When should we say no to new budget or headcount, and when should we support it?
How can we run prioritization as a continuous dialogue across multiple layers of the org?
search_terms: three frames of prioritization ambitions capacity investment
summary: This post argues that effective prioritization in product organizations is inherently difficult because it requires balancing ambition, constrained capacity, and constrained capital. It’s aimed at product and leadership teams who want a practical way to avoid common anti-patterns like ambition-only fantasy planning, capacity-only spreadsheet optimization, and investment-only “throw money at it” behavior. The core message is that prioritization must be an ongoing dialogue across organizational layers, not a one-time framework or quick fix.

title: TBM 374: Strategy & Urgency
questions: Which opportunities lose value if we wait, and which ones benefit from waiting?
Where can we safely land customers now with basics and expand later?
How do external deadlines force regrettable urgency, and what strategic options remain?
How can we shift from reactive urgency to proactive urgency that opens options later?
search_terms: cost of delay vs prioritization frameworks
strategy is timing and tempo control
RICE ICE MoSCoW WSJF urgency missing time
summary: The post argues that effective product strategy and prioritization require treating urgency as a first-class variable: how delay changes long-term value. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who feel stuck in reactive short-termism and want a way to reframe prioritization debates around timing, tempo, and competitive response windows. It also critiques common prioritization frameworks for not explicitly modeling the impact of time on outcomes.

title: TBM 352: Funding, Planning, and Context
questions: How should we plan and fund teams when they have different stages, dependencies, and proximity to revenue?
What goes wrong when we apply one budgeting/goal-setting model across all teams?
How do effort–value curves change how we should govern long-running or high-uncertainty work?
Should tech/product be treated as the business or as an enabling function for planning purposes?
search_terms: funding teams by stage and work shape
hill climbing model for organizational growth
summary: This post argues that planning and funding in product and tech organizations must be context-aware, because teams differ in stage, work shape, dependencies, and how value accrues. It provides a framework for diagnosing team contexts (including effort–value curves and exploration vs optimization) and highlights anti-patterns that emerge when organizations use rigid, certainty-seeking governance. It’s aimed at leaders and product/engineering managers who need a practical way to design funding and planning that stays legible without forcing a single monoprocess.

title: TBM 351: The 4 Prioritization Jobs (And Why It Matters)
questions: Which of the four prioritization jobs is causing the biggest pain in our organization right now?
How do we know which prioritization conversation we’re actually having in a given meeting or artifact?
Are our tradeoff discussions failing because of flow/WIP, unclear strategy, budget ambiguity, or decision-rights issues?
When we’re overwhelmed, should we stabilize execution first (limit WIP) or start with strategy and investment mix?
search_terms: prioritization conversations efficiency leverage autonomy funding
above the line below the line planning
summary: The post argues that “prioritization” usually bundles four different conversations—efficiency (flow/WIP), leverage/effectiveness (right bets), alignment/autonomy (shared context and decision rights), and support/funding/commitment (structural backing). It’s for product and engineering leaders and teams who struggle with overloaded work, unclear tradeoffs, political escalation, or dysfunctional budgeting processes, and want a clearer way to name and sequence the right prioritization job for their context.

title: TBM 312: Comparing Areas of Friction and Drag
questions: How do we turn a list of impediments into a prioritized plan to reduce friction?
Why do teams stop digging into blocker impact after raising issues?
Which friction sources should we address first based on severity, frequency, and impact over time?
How can we make a business case for fixing developer workflow problems?
search_terms: prioritizing tech debt vs workflow drag
summary: The post offers a facilitation tool for teams to compare and prioritize sources of developer friction and drag rather than stopping at venting. It helps product and engineering leaders structure discussions around severity, reach, frequency, predictability, mitigation difficulty, impact over time, and second-order effects. Intended for teams running internal workshops to build a business case for addressing impediments.

title: TBM 301: How to Reconcile Prioritization and $/Time Allocation
questions: Why do % allocation models fail to reflect real priorities and expected outcomes?
How can we reconcile prioritization with time and $ allocation in a way that improves execution?
What work styles should we use for tasks with different value and urgency profiles?
How do we make the assumptions behind allocations explicit and test them against reality?
search_terms: firefighting vs optimizers vs future seeds
CFO style budget vs actuals allocation pitfalls
friction vs smooth-flow value stream $1 equivalence
summary: The post argues that using percentage allocations of time or money as the primary strategy mechanism is misleading because it hides priorities, assumptions, and current blockers. It proposes treating allocations as an output to monitor—derived from a forced ranking of priorities and a value/urgency bucket model—so teams can choose appropriate work styles (support, bare minimum, optimizers, future seeds, and mop-up). It is aimed at leaders and operators trying to improve strategy execution and resource planning without getting trapped in spreadsheet-like %s.

title: TBM 285: Shared Teams and Apples-to-Oranges Priorities
questions: How should leadership set and compare multiple priority pillars that aren’t apples-to-apples?
What should a shared platform or enabling team do when multiple customer teams demand capacity with conflicting priorities?
How do you prevent trust erosion when shared teams are forced into unrealistic commitments?
What prioritization mechanism best balances autonomy for teams with fair access to shared capacity?
search_terms: how to prioritize competing GTM teams
summary: This post addresses a common product execution anti-pattern where leadership sets multiple equally weighted priority pillars that can’t be compared, causing shared dependency teams to absorb conflicting demands. It’s aimed at product leaders and managers who need practical ways to manage capacity, avoid trust erosion, and move from ad-hoc conflict toward clearer prioritization heuristics and more autonomous sequencing.

title: TBM 257: How to Make the Case for Slowing Down to Speed Up
questions: How do we make the business case for slowing down when we’re already moving slowly?
What should we include in a governance plan so leaders trust a long “slow down to speed up” effort?
How can we quantify the value of reducing drag, debt interest, and rework?
What is the best way to identify and pitch the wedge that unlocks progress?
search_terms: product and engineering governance for long projects
summary: This post is a practical guide for product and engineering leaders who need to persuade stakeholders to invest in “slow down to speed up” work (e.g., refactoring, stability, debt reduction) without relying on vague promises or big-bang rewrites. It focuses on building a credible business case using value metrics, blocked-work leverage, experiments to test assumptions, and governance that de-risks progress. It’s aimed at teams struggling to get buy-in when near-term output is the dominant priority.

title: TBM 245: The Magic Prioritization Trick
questions: How do we prioritize initiatives when urgency and value are often misaligned?
What is the qualitative cost of delay for different initiatives?
How should duration be incorporated to sequence work effectively?
Why do teams overestimate urgency and delay strategic prioritization?
search_terms: weighted cost of delay CoD duration
summary: The post presents a simple workshop “prioritization trick” for teams to decouple urgency from value and then incorporate duration to sequence initiatives using cost of delay. It’s aimed at product, engineering, and delivery teams that struggle with misaligned urgency/value judgments and want a practical method for ordering work. The approach also explicitly leverages cognitive bias awareness to make prioritization more accurate.

title: TBM 232: Leverage and Benefit Types
questions: How do I categorize initiatives by benefit type: increase revenue, protect revenue, reduce costs, or avoid costs?
What makes an initiative high leverage versus low leverage?
Why does reactive cost cutting often end up shifting expenses rather than eliminating them?
How can we pursue revenue growth while also protecting revenue and managing costs under constraints?
search_terms: benefit types increase revenue protect revenue reduce costs avoid costs
high leverage initiatives vs low leverage bets
employee retention as cost and revenue enabler
summary: The post discusses a value framework that breaks benefits into four types—revenue growth, revenue protection, cost reduction, and cost avoidance—and argues that this framework is incomplete without considering leverage. It’s aimed at leaders and operators trying to prioritize initiatives in capital- and budget-constrained environments, especially when they need to balance growth with cost discipline and resilience. The core emphasis is that high-leverage infrastructure and retention efforts can unlock multiple value drivers, while reactive bets tend to shift burdens to the future.

title: TBM 220: Effort vs. Value Curves
questions: How do we estimate effort vs. value over time instead of relying on level of effort?
What curve shape best describes our work (sigmoid, exponential decay, linear, step, J-curve, delayed step)?
Why do “quick wins” feel different in practice depending on the underlying value curve?
When should we expect results, and what leading indicators can signal progress before completion?
search_terms: prioritization framework critique level of effort
diminishing returns exponential decay UX improvements
summary: The post argues that product teams should stop treating prioritization as a single-point level-of-effort problem and instead model how effort/time translates into value over time. It introduces several effort-vs-value curve archetypes (sigmoid, exponential decay, linear, step, J-curve, delayed step) and explains how each changes expectations, risk, and the meaning of “quick wins.” It’s aimed at teams using prioritization frameworks who want a more realistic way to plan focus areas and set time-to-results.

title: TBM 217: Why Are You Doing This? (Wrong Answers Only)
questions: What are we working on right now, and why is it the most important thing we could be doing?
Is our chosen metric the right one and the highest-leverage way to move customer outcomes?
Are we ignoring opportunity costs due to sunk costs, dependencies, or backlog inertia?
How do we balance short-term wins with long-term sustainable growth and risk-adjusted upside?
search_terms: TBM 217 why are you doing this
sunk cost fallacy in product decisions
summary: This post offers a practical leadership and team decision framework for interrogating why a project is the top priority, using a 'wrong answers only' prompt to surface weak justifications. It is aimed at leaders and product/engineering managers who need to improve prioritization quality by focusing on customer value, leverage, opportunity cost, and how decisions hold up as context changes.

title: TBM 16/52: Can Do vs. Should Do
questions: Is our team prioritizing what we should do or only what we can do?
Which types of work are we stuck in because of blockers, dependencies, or organizational constraints?
Are we mistaking effort/complexity for impact, and what are we missing by doing so?
When we can’t directly change something, are we using influence actions effectively?
search_terms: team introspection can do should do
summary: The post proposes a nine-box framework for evaluating product and team work by separating what is possible (“can”) from what is strategically right (“should”). It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who struggle with misprioritization, burnout from unsustainable work, and getting stuck in low-value or blocked activities. The core guidance is to use “can” work to unblock “should” work and to apply the right kind of action when direct change isn’t possible.

title: TBM 43/52: The Secret to Initiative Success (D, LC, F, EC)
questions: How do I identify the drivers, limiting constraints, and floats for a new initiative?
When should I add enabling constraints instead of relying on flexibility (floats)?
What is the risk of optimizing for too many goals and constraints at the same time?
How can I structure cross-team agreements to increase the odds of initiative success?
search_terms: Rothman Manage It drivers constraints floats
summary: This post offers a practical framework for improving initiative outcomes by mapping drivers (goals), limiting constraints (hard limits), floats (flexibility), and enabling constraints (intentional narrowing that helps progress). It’s aimed at product teams and product managers who struggle with paralysis, misalignment, or “success theater” when they try to optimize too many factors at once. The core guidance is to reduce the number of drivers and limiting constraints, clarify floats, and use enabling constraints to counterbalance flexibility and coordinate execution.

title: TBM 19/53: Drivers, Constraints, and Floats
questions: What are the drivers, constraints, and floats in my current effort?
Which constraints are actually going to matter, and which are just assumed?
How can I narrow and sharpen my drivers to avoid getting stuck in tradeoff loops?
Where can I find or create floats to increase flexibility and improve outcomes?
search_terms: Johanna Rothman Manage It drivers constraints floats
product planning tradeoffs drivers constraints floats
project management triangle good fast cheap choose two
how to identify constraints that matter
summary: The post explains a practical planning mental model—drivers, constraints, and floats—for product and initiative work where multiple goals compete. It argues that teams should minimize drivers and constraints while increasing flexibility, and it provides guidance on the real challenges of identifying and narrowing these elements. It’s aimed at product leaders and practitioners who want a clearer way to manage tradeoffs and execution risk.

title: TBM 14/53: 1s and 3s
questions: How can we connect day-to-day work to company-level goals across multiple time horizons?
What time-scale framework helps teams avoid over-precision while still maintaining coherence?
Where do our plans become unclear between sprint work and executive-level strategy?
How do we ensure work is traceable end-to-end rather than siloed by team or sprint?
search_terms: bet traceability near to long term
summary: The post proposes a simple planning framework using connected 1-3 ranges of time (hours through decades) to help teams link their immediate work to longer-term company bets. It is aimed at teams struggling with alignment and coherence, especially in the often unclear 1-3 month to 1-3 quarter “messy middle.”

title: TBM 13/53: Good Idea?
questions: How do we prioritize product ideas based on impact rather than features?
What should a judging/ranking guide include so it leads to better decisions?
How can we avoid faux rigor from spreadsheet-based prioritization?
How do we structure facilitation to generate productive product conversations?
summary: This post argues that product teams should replace feature-centric brainstorming with an impact-focused judging/ranking guide that makes evaluation criteria explicit and conversation-driven. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who struggle with spreadsheet theater, want better facilitation methods, and need a reusable way to compare ideas using impact, confidence, strategic alignment, and learning goals.
